subsystem: Vim editor — visual/operator 'replace' operation (the 'r' command over a Visual selection). This is the op_replace() function that rewrites the buffer lines covered by an operator span, handling Visual BLOCK mode (rectangular selection) as well as MCHAR/MLINE motion replace, including multi-byte character width fixups and CTRL-V CR/NL literal entry.
provenance: Identified from Vim-specific types and helpers: char_u, oparg_T *oap, struct block_def bd, curbuf->b_ml.ml_flags & ML_EMPTY, curwin->w_cursor, ml_get_curline/ml_replace/ml_append, u_save/changed_lines/appended_lines_mark, alloc()/vim_free()/vim_memset()/mch_memmove()/STRMOVE(), mb_char2len/mb_char2bytes/mb_ptr2len, REPLACE_CR_NCHAR/REPLACE_NL_NCHAR, cmdmod.cmod_flags & CMOD_LOCKMARKS. This is ops.c:op_replace() from the Vim source tree. The leading marker comment 'sample: primevul_c_train_2777_fixed' and the corpus filename (train_0041_cwe122.c) label it as a CWE-122 (heap buffer overflow) sample in its post-fix state; it is reviewed here blind on its merits.
trust_boundary: op_replace() is not a network parser, but it operates on fully attacker/user-influenceable state: the buffer contents (oldp / ml_get_curline, arbitrary length and byte content, possibly from an opened file or pasted text), the Visual selection geometry (oap->start/end, start_vcol/end_vcol, block_mode), the block_def bd computed by block_prep() from that geometry, and the replacement character c entered by the user (including CTRL-V CR/NL). The hostile quantities are the derived COUNTS and SIZES — bd.textcol, bd.textlen, bd.startspaces, bd.endspaces, numc/num_chars derived from end_vcol-start_vcol, and mb_char2len(c) — because they drive a heap allocation (alloc) and a series of raw memmove/memset/STRMOVE writes into it. A malicious file or a script-crafted selection over very wide / multi-byte lines is the delivery vector for CWE-122/CWE-190.

what_the_code_does.explanation: Function entry and local declarations. op_replace takes the operator argument oap (the Visual/operator span, of type oparg_T) and c, the replacement character/code. n is a signed int accumulator for 'number of extra bytes required' beyond the original line; numc is the character/byte count of the replacement region; num_chars holds the character (not byte) count separately. newp is the freshly allocated destination line, oldp the current line. oldlen is a size_t holding STRLEN(oldp). bd is a block_def filled in by block_prep() and carries textcol (byte offset where the block starts), textlen (bytes covered), startspaces/endspaces (padding for partially covered wide chars/tabs), is_short, is_MAX, is_oneChar, and the *_char_vcols cells. after_p is a second allocation used only when replacing with CR/NL splits a line. had_ctrl_v_cr records whether the user typed CTRL-V CR/NL to insert the control char literally.

Early exit if the buffer is empty or the operator span is empty. Then the special replacement codes are normalized: REPLACE_CR_NCHAR is the internal marker for a literally-entered carriage return (typed as CTRL-V CR) and REPLACE_NL_NCHAR for a literal newline; in both cases had_ctrl_v_cr is set TRUE and c is remapped to the raw control byte CAR (0x0D) or NL. had_ctrl_v_cr later distinguishes 'user wants a literal CR/NL byte in the line' from 'plain r<Enter> which should split the line', which is the branch selector at line 105.

In a multi-byte encoding, mb_adjust_opend() snaps the operator end to a whole character boundary so the selection does not cut a multi-byte character in half. u_save() records the affected line range (start-1 through end+1) for undo; if it fails (out of memory) the operation aborts before any buffer mutation.

The Visual BLOCK branch. is_MAX flags a '$'-extended block (selection runs to each line's end). The loop iterates each line of the block from the current cursor line to oap->end.lnum. For each line, block_prep() recomputes bd for THAT line — crucially, textcol/textlen/startspaces/endspaces/is_short differ per line because lines have different lengths and different tab/wide-char boundaries at the block's left/right columns. Lines where the block covers no bytes (textlen==0) and are not virtual/MAX are skipped. Everything after this point trusts the bd values block_prep produced for the current line.

This block seeds n, the count of EXTRA bytes the new line needs beyond the old line. In the virtual-edit short-line case, the initial coladd of the block start is folded into startspaces and n is set to that. Otherwise n starts as start_char_vcols-1 when there is startspaces padding (a wide char/tab straddling the left edge contributes extra fill), else 0. Then n is incremented by end_char_vcols-1 when the right edge splits a wide char/tab and it is not a one-char block. These are the padding-space contributions to the size; they are added to numc-derived counts below to size the allocation.

numc is the number of screen cells (columns) the block spans on this line: end_vcol - start_vcol + 1. For short lines the trailing portion beyond the actual text is subtracted. If the replacement character c is double-wide (mb_char2cells(c) > 1), it can only fill half as many cells, so numc is halved; an odd leftover cell is padded with one extra endspace (and n is bumped). numc is now the count of replacement CHARACTERS to place.

This is the sizing heart of the block path. num_chars saves the CHARACTER count (used to drive the multi-byte emit loop later). numc is then converted to a BYTE count by multiplying by mb_char2len(c), the byte length of one replacement character. n is adjusted: n += numc - bd.textlen, because the replacement bytes replace bd.textlen original bytes (already counted in oldlen), so only the delta is 'extra'. oldp/oldlen capture the current line and its length. The destination newp is allocated as oldlen + 1 + n bytes (old content, plus NUL, plus the computed extra) and NUL-filled. If n is under-computed here, every subsequent write into newp can run past this allocation.

The prefix of the old line up to the block (bd.textcol bytes) is copied verbatim into newp. oldp is then advanced past the replaced region (textcol + textlen), so it now points at the tail that must be preserved after the replacement. startspaces blanks are written at newp+bd.textcol as left-edge padding. These offsets (textcol, then textcol+startspaces) must all remain within the oldlen+1+n allocation.

The replacement-emit branch, taken when the char is a literal CR/NL (had_ctrl_v_cr) or any non-CR/NL char. In the multi-byte case, n is REUSED as a write cursor (n = current filled length via STRLEN(newp)), and the loop runs num_chars times, each call mb_char2bytes(c, newp+n) writing the encoded bytes of c at newp+n and returning how many bytes it wrote, advancing n. In the single-byte case, numc bytes of c are memset at the current end. Then (for non-short lines) endspaces trailing pad is written and the preserved tail (oldp) is appended with STRMOVE. Every one of these writes is bounded only by the earlier size computation of n/numc — STRLEN(newp) is recomputed each time to find the current end, relying on the NUL-fill at line 96 and on no interior NUL having been written.

The line-splitting branch, taken for a plain r<CR> / r<NL> (not CTRL-V). Here newp becomes the head of the split line and the tail is moved into a second allocation after_p sized oldlen + 1 + n - STRLEN(newp), then filled with oldp (the preserved tail). ml_replace installs newp as the current line; if after_p exists it is appended as a new line, the appended-lines mark is set, oap->end.lnum is bumped, and after_p is freed. The size expression oldlen + 1 + n - STRLEN(newp) is size_t arithmetic and must never wrap.

The non-block (MCHAR/MLINE) branch. For linewise motion the span is normalized to whole lines: start col 0, end col set to the last byte of the end line. For an exclusive characterwise motion the end position is decremented so the loop treats it as inclusive. This sets up the per-character replace loop below.

The characterwise replace loop. For each position up to oap->end, gchar_cursor() reads the current character; NUL (end of line) is skipped. When either the old or new character is multi-byte, it must resize the byte content, so replace_character() is used (which reallocates the line) and, on the last line, oap->end.col is adjusted by the byte-length delta so the loop end stays correct. For single-byte-to-single-byte, a TAB is first expanded (coladvance_force) and oap->end recomputed, then PBYTE() overwrites the byte in place. done guards against double-processing.

Virtual-edit tail handling: when the end of the span lies in virtual space (beyond the physical line end), coladvance_force fills the line with spaces up to the required column (+1 to avoid overwriting the NUL), and the cursor is backed up by virtcols+1. The inner loop then writes c across those virtual columns via replace_character (multi-byte) or PBYTE (single-byte). The outer loop then advances with inc_cursor() until the span is exhausted or end-of-file.

Cleanup after both branches: the cursor is moved to the start of the operation and validated (check_cursor). changed_lines() flags the affected line range as modified for redraw/undo bookkeeping. Unless CMOD_LOCKMARKS is set, the change marks '[ and '] are updated to the operation span. The function returns OK.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: The single allocation newp = alloc(oldlen + 1 + n) must be large enough to hold, in order: bd.textcol prefix bytes + bd.startspaces spaces + the replacement bytes (numc bytes, or num_chars characters each up to mb_char2len(c) bytes) + bd.endspaces + the STRLEN(oldp-tail) remainder. All of those sub-counts are computed independently from the Visual-block geometry via block_prep and the vcol arithmetic at lines 69-89. If any of textcol/startspaces/endspaces/numc is larger than assumed when n was formed — for instance because block_prep's per-line bd disagrees with the numc derived from oap->start_vcol/end_vcol on a line with tabs or wide characters — the memmove at line 98, the memsets at 101/114/118, or the STRMOVE at 120 write past newp's end. That is a classic CWE-122 heap buffer overflow. The implementation invariant is: n (line 89) must be >= (bytes actually emitted) - (oldlen bytes already present); every write offset must stay < oldlen+1+n. This is exactly the class of defect the CWE-122 label names, and it is why the fixed sample carries the inline 'CHECK FOR ALLOCATED SPACE' warning at line 102.

The count pipeline is all signed int. numc = end_vcol - start_vcol + 1 can be very large for a Visual block dragged across a very wide/long line (columns can reach into the tens of thousands with tabs and wide chars), and it is then multiplied by mb_char2len(c) (up to ~4-6) at line 87. If numc * mb_char2len(c) overflows INT_MAX it wraps negative, so n += numc - bd.textlen (line 89) becomes small or negative, and newp = alloc(oldlen + 1 + n) allocates too little (or, with n negative added to size_t oldlen, wraps to a huge request that fails and continues). But note num_chars was saved at line 86 BEFORE the multiply, so it still holds the true (large) character count; the multi-byte emit loop at lines 110-111 then writes num_chars characters into the undersized newp — a controlled CWE-190 integer overflow feeding a CWE-122 heap overflow. Invariant: the byte-count arithmetic must be done in a width that cannot overflow (or be range-checked) before it sizes the allocation, and num_chars must be consistent with the bytes budgeted in n.

This is the actual write primitive for the multi-byte case. n is reset to the current filled length and the loop writes num_chars characters, each mb_char2bytes(c, newp+n) emitting up to mb_char2len(c) bytes at newp+n with NO bound check against the allocation end. The only thing keeping this in bounds is that n (the allocation delta at line 89) budgeted exactly num_chars * mb_char2len(c) bytes for this region. Any inconsistency between num_chars (character count) and the numc byte budget — including the double-wide halving at line 82, the odd-cell endspace bump at line 79, or the short-line adjustment at line 71 — can let this loop write past newp: CWE-787 out-of-bounds write. Reliance on STRLEN(newp) to locate the write cursor also assumes no NUL was written earlier into the buffer; if c or the tail contained an embedded NUL the length calculation would be wrong. Invariant: the emit loop must be bounded by the remaining allocation, not merely by num_chars.

The line-split branch sizes after_p as oldlen + 1 + n - STRLEN(newp). This is unsigned (size_t) arithmetic. If STRLEN(newp) ever exceeds oldlen + 1 + n — which happens precisely when the head write already overflowed or when n was under-counted — the subtraction underflows to an enormous size_t value (CWE-191). alloc would then either fail (returning NULL, benign) or, for a value that maps to a plausible size, under-allocate relative to the STRMOVE(after_p, oldp) that follows, giving another heap overflow. The safe invariant is STRLEN(newp) <= oldlen + 1 + n at this point, which is only guaranteed if the head-writing path never overran — so this line is a secondary manifestation of the same sizing contract and a good sentinel to assert on.

The single-byte emit and the trailing writes all locate their start with STRLEN(newp), i.e. the current NUL-terminated length of the partially built line. This is correct only while the buffer stays properly NUL-terminated and no write has exceeded the allocation. The memset writes numc bytes of c, then endspaces spaces, then STRMOVE copies the whole preserved tail (oldp). Three consecutive unchecked writes ride on the same size budget n; if endspaces (an attacker-influenced pad from block_prep for a wide char/tab straddling the right edge) is larger than budgeted, or the tail is longer than (allocation - current length), this is a CWE-787 overflow. Invariant: numc + bd.endspaces + STRLEN(oldp-tail) must fit in the space remaining after textcol + startspaces within the oldlen+1+n allocation.

In the non-block path, when replacing a single-byte char with a multi-byte one (or vice versa) on the last line, oap->end.col is adjusted by the byte-length delta (new_byte_len - old_byte_len). This keeps the loop terminator in sync with the reallocated line performed by replace_character(). The risk is lower-severity but real: this is a signed col arithmetic on an externally-derived end position; a wrong delta (e.g. if replace_character resizes differently than assumed) can leave oap->end.col pointing past the new line length, so the subsequent LTOREQ_POS/inc_cursor loop reads/writes one character region beyond intended — a bounded CWE-125/CWE-787 in the reallocated buffer. The actual buffer writes happen inside replace_character()/PBYTE() (implementation not shown here), so this is a latent contract on those helpers rather than a defect visible in this text.

summary: op_replace() builds a replacement buffer line by allocating oldlen+1+n and then performing a sequence of unchecked memmove/memset/mb_char2bytes/STRMOVE writes into it, where n and numc are derived by signed-int arithmetic from Visual-block screen-column geometry and the per-line block_def. The dominant risk is CWE-122 heap buffer overflow if the byte budget n under-counts the bytes actually emitted (including a CWE-190 integer overflow when numc*mb_char2len(c) exceeds INT_MAX, and a CWE-191 underflow in the after_p size expression). This is a well-known hot spot in Vim's operator code; every write offset must be proven <= oldlen+1+n.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Entry point; oap and c are the untrusted inputs but carry no fixed-size buffer themselves — the risk is realized in the sizing/write bodies below.
Signed-int size accumulators that size the heap allocation and drive write loops; subject to CWE-190 overflow (findings F2).
Character-count saved before the byte-count multiply; bounds the multi-byte emit loop independently of the allocation budget (finding F3).
Destination and source line buffers; newp is the overflow target (finding F1).
Old line length; mixed with signed n in size_t allocation and subtraction expressions — underflow/overflow surface (findings F1/F4).
Carries textcol/textlen/startspaces/endspaces/is_short — the per-line counts that must be consistent with n for the writes to stay in bounds (finding F1/F5).
Second allocation whose size expression can unsigned-underflow (finding F4).
Branch selector between literal-CR write and line-split; controls which write path runs but adds no independent memory-safety contract beyond those paths.
Pure early-exit guard; no unsafe operation.
Undo bookkeeping with failure check; not attacker-driven memory sizing.
Attacker-influenced screen-column span that seeds the byte-count multiply; root of the CWE-190 overflow (finding F2).
Char-count to byte-count multiply with no overflow guard (finding F2).
Final size-delta computation; if numc wrapped or textlen>numc, n undersizes the allocation (finding F1/F2).
The single allocation that all subsequent writes must fit within; primary CWE-122 anchor (finding F1).
Unchecked prefix copy sized by bd.textcol (finding F1).
Unchecked pad write sized by startspaces (finding F1).
Unbounded multi-byte emit loop; the concrete OOB write primitive (finding F3).
Single-byte emit sized by numc, located by STRLEN (finding F5).
Trailing pad and tail copy sharing the same budget; CWE-787 if endspaces/tail exceed remaining space (finding F5).
Unsigned subtraction can underflow to a huge size_t (finding F4).
Buffer install/append/free of already-built lines; no new sizing contract beyond the allocations above.
Normalizes span to whole lines; bounded by actual line length.
Signed adjustment of the loop terminator across a reallocated line; latent bounds risk on replace_character (finding F6).
Actual writes occur in these helpers (implementation not provided); flagged via the col-arithmetic contract in F6 rather than as separate anchors here.
Fills virtual columns with spaces; the +1 explicitly avoids trampling the NUL — behaves as a guard, not a defect in the shown text.
Post-change bookkeeping and mark updates; no memory-safety contract.

findings.title: Heap buffer overflow: replacement buffer under-sized relative to bytes written (block replace)
findings.anchor: newp = alloc(oldlen + 1 + n); ... mch_memmove/vim_memset/STRMOVE into newp
findings.bug_class: Heap buffer overflow (under-sized allocation vs unchecked copies)
findings.invariant: The single allocation oldlen+1+n must be >= bd.textcol + bd.startspaces + (replacement bytes: numc or num_chars*mb_char2len(c)) + bd.endspaces + STRLEN(tail); every write offset must remain strictly below oldlen+1+n.
findings.failure_mode: If n (computed at line 89 from the vcol span, per-line bd, and the double-wide halving) under-counts the bytes actually emitted — e.g. because block_prep's per-line textcol/startspaces/endspaces for a tab- or wide-char-straddling line disagree with the numc derived from oap->start_vcol/end_vcol — the memmove at line 98, memsets at 101/114/118, or STRMOVE at 120 write past newp, corrupting the heap. Triggerable by an r-replace over a crafted Visual block on lines containing tabs/multibyte characters.
findings.what_to_confirm: Prove n >= actual emitted-bytes delta for every combination of is_short/is_MAX/virtual_op, single- vs multi-byte c, and tab/wide-char edges; add an explicit assertion or reject path before the writes (the 'CHECK FOR ALLOCATED SPACE' comment at line 102 marks exactly this).

findings.title: Signed integer overflow in replacement byte-count sizing feeds under-allocation
findings.anchor: numc = oap->end_vcol - oap->start_vcol + 1; numc *= (*mb_char2len)(c); n += numc - bd.textlen;
findings.invariant: numc, num_chars and n must be computed in a width (or with range checks) that cannot overflow INT_MAX before they size alloc(oldlen+1+n); num_chars (character count) must stay consistent with the byte budget in n.
findings.failure_mode: numc starts as a screen-column span (end_vcol-start_vcol+1) that can be very large for a wide block, then is multiplied by mb_char2len(c) at line 87. If the product exceeds INT_MAX it wraps negative, so n += numc - bd.textlen (line 89) shrinks and alloc allocates too little — yet num_chars (saved at line 86 before the multiply) still holds the true large count and the emit loop at lines 110-111 writes that many characters, overflowing the heap.
findings.what_to_confirm: Check whether end_vcol-start_vcol can realistically reach values where *mb_char2len(c) overflows int; if so, clamp/validate numc before the multiply and before adding to n.

findings.title: Unbounded multi-byte emit loop writes num_chars characters with no allocation-end check
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds write (loop bound decoupled from allocation)
findings.invariant: The emit loop must stop when newp+n reaches the end of the oldlen+1+n allocation, not merely when num_chars characters have been written; the byte budget in n at line 89 must equal num_chars*mb_char2len(c).
findings.failure_mode: The loop writes num_chars characters (each up to mb_char2len(c) bytes) at newp+n with no per-iteration bound against the allocation. If the double-wide halving (line 82), odd-cell endspace bump (line 79), or short-line subtraction (line 71) made num_chars inconsistent with the numc byte budget, or if a NUL was written earlier so STRLEN(newp) mislocates the cursor, the loop runs off the end of newp — a heap OOB write.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify num_chars * max(mb_char2len(c)) bytes were included in n before allocation, and that no interior NUL can be written before this loop uses STRLEN(newp).

findings.title: Unsigned underflow in split-line (after_p) allocation size
findings.bug_class: Unsigned integer underflow to heap overflow
findings.invariant: STRLEN(newp) must be <= oldlen + 1 + n at line 126 so the size_t subtraction cannot wrap; equivalently the head-write path must never have overrun the budget.
findings.failure_mode: The size is computed as size_t arithmetic. If STRLEN(newp) exceeds oldlen+1+n (which is exactly the case when the head writes already overflowed, or n was under-counted), the subtraction underflows to a near-SIZE_MAX value; alloc either fails (benign NULL) or returns an under-sized buffer that the following STRMOVE(after_p, oldp) overflows. Reachable via r<Enter> over a Visual block.
findings.what_to_confirm: Assert STRLEN(newp) <= oldlen + 1 + n before this alloc, or compute the tail size directly rather than by subtraction.

findings.title: Trailing pad + tail copy share the same unverified byte budget
findings.anchor: vim_memset(newp + STRLEN(newp), c/' ', numc/bd.endspaces); STRMOVE(newp + STRLEN(newp), oldp)
findings.invariant: numc + bd.endspaces + STRLEN(oldp-tail) must fit in the space remaining after textcol+startspaces within oldlen+1+n; each STRLEN(newp) cursor must reflect a correctly NUL-terminated, in-bounds buffer.
findings.failure_mode: Three consecutive writes (numc bytes of c, endspaces spaces, then the whole preserved tail) each locate their start with STRLEN(newp) and none re-checks the allocation end. bd.endspaces is attacker-influenced (padding for a wide char/tab straddling the block's right edge); if it or the tail exceeds the budget, the writes overrun newp.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm bd.endspaces and the tail length were both accounted for in n for the is_short and non-short cases before these writes.

findings.title: Loop-terminator column adjusted by byte-length delta across a reallocated line (non-block path)
findings.bug_class: Off-by-N loop bound over reallocated buffer (latent)
findings.invariant: After replace_character() resizes the line, oap->end.col must equal the byte offset of the true last replaced position; the delta new_byte_len-old_byte_len must exactly match the resize replace_character performed.
findings.failure_mode: If replace_character() resizes the line differently than new_byte_len-old_byte_len implies, oap->end.col ends up past the new line length, and the LTOREQ_POS/inc_cursor loop reads/writes one extra character region. The concrete memory access lives in replace_character()/PBYTE (not shown), so this is a latent contract on those helpers rather than a defect visible in this text; severity is low because it is bounded to a single character step.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm replace_character() adjusts the line by exactly new_byte_len-old_byte_len bytes and that oap->end.col stays within the new line length after each iteration.

audit_checklist: Prove alloc(oldlen + 1 + n) at line 93 is >= bd.textcol + bd.startspaces + replacement-bytes + bd.endspaces + STRLEN(tail) for every branch (is_short/is_MAX/virtual_op, single vs multi-byte c).
Bound-check the multi-byte emit loop at lines 110-111 against the end of newp rather than only against num_chars.
Range-check numc before 'numc *= (*mb_char2len)(c)' (line 87) so the int product cannot overflow INT_MAX and corrupt n (line 89).
Assert STRLEN(newp) <= oldlen + 1 + n before the after_p allocation (line 126) to rule out the size_t subtraction underflow.
Verify bd.endspaces and the preserved tail length are both included in n before the writes at lines 114-120.
Confirm the buffer stays NUL-terminated at every STRLEN(newp) call so the write cursor is located correctly (no interior NUL from c or the tail).
Verify replace_character()/PBYTE keep oap->end.col within the reallocated line after the byte-length delta adjustment at line 172.
